title: 1 Corinthians
author: Paul, the apostle, with Sosthenes, from Ephesus
setting: The church at Corinth — a prosperous, cosmopolitan, deeply divided congregation in a major port city. Paul founded the church on his second missionary journey and now writes to address a series of reported problems.
purpose: To address multiple crises in the Corinthian church: divisions, sexual immorality, lawsuits, food offered to idols, the misuse of spiritual gifts, and confusion about the resurrection — all rooted in an over-realized eschatology and worldly wisdom.
themes: The cross as the wisdom and power of God (chs. 1–4)
Sexual ethics and the body as God's temple
Marriage, singleness, and the coming Lord
Order in worship and the Lord's Supper
The resurrection as the foundation of Christian hope (ch. 15)
Spiritual gifts used for edification, not display

outline.label: Divisions and the Wisdom of the Cross
Food Offered to Idols; Rights and Freedom
Spiritual Gifts, Love, and Worship Order

before.label: Paul founds the Corinthian church during his second journey
A previous letter (now lost) precedes this one
Chloe's people bring disturbing news of divisions

after.label: Jerusalem Council — Jewish-Gentile tensions resolved apostolically
Paul's painful visit to Corinth follows this letter's failure
2 Corinthians — Paul defends his ministry; reconciliation

key_people.name: Paul
key_people.role: Founder of the Corinthian church who writes to address divisions and disorders that have developed in his absence — the letter reveals more about life in an early church than any other NT document

key_people.name: Apollos
key_people.role: The eloquent Alexandrian teacher (Acts 18:24) whose ministry at Corinth has contributed to the party spirit; Paul carefully defuses the competition between himself and Apollos

key_people.name: Chloe's People
key_people.role: The unnamed messengers who bring Paul the news of the Corinthian divisions — 1:11 is the book's occasion statement, making the letter's context unusually transparent

context: 1 Corinthians was written c. AD 54-55 from Ephesus during Paul's third missionary journey. Corinth was Rome's wealthiest Greek city, a port renowned for commerce, sexual immorality, and social stratification. The church Paul founded there reflected the city's problems: faction, status competition, sexual license, and theological confusion. The letter addresses a long list of specific issues and reveals the complexity of applying the gospel to a highly stratified, cosmopolitan Roman city.
christ_connection: The cross is 1 Corinthians' recurring reference point for every pastoral problem: party spirit is overcome when the church is centred on the crucified Christ rather than on eloquent teachers (1:18-2:5); sexual immorality is addressed by the fact that our bodies are temples of the Spirit and members of Christ (6:15-20); the Lord's Supper is a proclamation of the Lord's death (11:26); resurrection hope is grounded entirely in Christ's resurrection as the firstfruits (15:20-23). Paul's pastoral method is consistently christocentric: the specific problems of the Corinthian church are addressed by returning to the implications of who Christ is and what he has done.

key_verses.note: 'The word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God' — the paradigm that overturns Corinthian status-wisdom

The love chapter's description of agape: 'Love is patient and kind... bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things' — the character of love that transcends every spiritual gift
'But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' — the resurrection as the ground of all Christian hope, against those who denied it at Corinth

themes_detail.title: The Wisdom of the Cross versus the Wisdom of the World
themes_detail.text: The opening four chapters address the party spirit by exposing its theological root: Corinthian Christians are evaluating their teachers by the standards of Greco-Roman rhetoric and social status rather than by the gospel. Paul's response is radical: God's wisdom is the crucified Christ (1:24), which is folly by every human standard. This does not mean Christianity is anti-intellectual but that the cross reorders the hierarchy of values: the impressive, the powerful, the noble are not God's preferred instruments (1:26-29). Paul's own manner of speaking (2:1-5) is a deliberate embodiment of this principle.

themes_detail.title: The Body of Christ and Spiritual Unity in Diversity
themes_detail.text: The image of the church as a body (ch. 12) is Paul's answer to Corinthian status-competition over spiritual gifts. The Spirit distributes gifts as he wills (12:11), and every member — including the ones who seem least honourable — is necessary and valuable (12:22-24). The church's diversity (many gifts, many members) is coordinated by the Spirit toward a unified end (one body, one Lord, one Spirit). Chapter 13 interrupts the gifts discussion to identify love as the way that gives every gift its value: without love, even the highest gifts are nothing.

themes_detail.title: Resurrection as the Foundation of Christian Hope and Ethics
themes_detail.text: Chapter 15 is the longest treatment of the resurrection in the NT, addressing Corinthians who denied a future bodily resurrection. Paul's argument moves from historical evidence (Christ appeared to Peter, the Twelve, 500 brethren, James, Paul — 15:5-8) to theological logic (if there is no resurrection, Christ is not risen; if Christ is not risen, faith is vain; if faith is vain, you are still in your sins — 15:13-17) to eschatological vision (the resurrection body, the transformation of the dead, death's defeat at the last enemy — 15:35-57). The resurrection is not a detachable doctrinal bonus but the load-bearing wall of the entire structure.

themes_detail.title: Christian Freedom and Its Limits
themes_detail.text: Several sections of the letter deal with the principle 'all things are lawful but not all things are beneficial' (6:12; 10:23). The issue is how Christian freedom should be governed in the context of the community: freedom to eat food sacrificed to idols must be limited by concern for the weaker brother's conscience (chs. 8-10); freedom regarding sexual relations is limited by the body's status as a temple of the Spirit (6:12-20) and by the call to holiness (7:1-40). Paul's consistent principle is that Christian freedom is exercised in the service of love — the freedom to give up one's rights for the sake of another, as Christ gave up his glory for us.
